commands.author: LiLan0125

target.title: fix(agents): skip A2A announce flow for isolated cron sessions_send
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolated cron sessions calling `sessions_send` had the target agent's reply injected back into the cron run via the A2A ping-pong loop, causing session state corruption and feedback loops (#92257).
- This change detects isolated cron requesters using the canonical `isCronRunSessionKey` parser (from `session-key-utils.ts`) and skips the A2A announce flow — the same skip pattern already used for ACP and native subagent sessions.
- The canonical parser only matches `agent:<id>:cron:<job>:run:<run>` keys and correctly rejects non-canonical `:cron:` segments (e.g. `agent:main:slack:cron:job:run:uuid`).
- Normal (non-cron) `sessions_send` callers still go through the full A2A ping-pong + announce path.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/tools/sessions-send-*` → **38/38 passed** (4 test files)
- Canonical classifier verified with 6 test cases via `node --import tsx` calling `isCronRunSessionKey` directly from source

## Impact Assessment

- Scope: `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` — adds one skip condition to the existing `skipA2AFlow` gate
- Downstream: no API or config changes; uses the same canonical parser as other cron session checks
- Edge cases: non-canonical `:cron:` keys are correctly excluded by the parsed classifier
- Detector: updated from raw `:cron:` substring check to `isCronRunSessionKey` per ClawSweeper review (P1 finding)

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Isolated cron `sessions_send` A2A ping-pong injected target replies into the cron session. After the fix, cron requesters skip the A2A flow entirely — no reply context leaks back into the cron run.

**Environment tested:** Node 22.x on Linux, vitest test suite + `node --import tsx` production function verification.

**Steps run after the patch:** Ran sessions-send test suites and verified the canonical `isCronRunSessionKey` classifier against positive and negative cases.

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { isCronRunSessionKey } from './src/sessions/session-key-utils.js';
const tests = [
  ['agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6', true, 'canonical cron run'],
  ['agent:main:cron:job-1:run:abc123', true, 'cron job run'],
  ['agent:main:slack:cron:job:run:uuid', false, 'non-canonical slack+cron'],
  ['agent:main:main', false, 'main session'],
  [null, false, 'null'],
  [undefined, false, 'undefined'],
];
for (const [key, expected, label] of tests) {
  console.log(isCronRunSessionKey(key) === expected ? 'PASS' : 'FAIL', '|', label, '|', JSON.stringify(key));
}
"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
PASS | canonical cron run | "agent:youtube:cron:bf05dd0f:run:b41f8df6"
PASS | cron job run | "agent:main:cron:job-1:run:abc123"
PASS | non-canonical slack+cron | "agent:main:slack:cron:job:run:uuid"
PASS | main session | "agent:main:main"
PASS | null | null
PASS | undefined | undefined
```

**Observed result:** The canonical `isCronRunSessionKey` parser correctly identifies real cron run sessions while rejecting non-canonical `:cron:` segments. All 38 existing tests pass with the updated detector.

**Not tested:** Live gateway with a cron job triggering sessions_send was not exercised.

Closes #92257

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(voice-call): add onBlockReply to runEmbeddedAgent for early TTS
target.body:
## Root Cause

When `generateVoiceResponse()` calls `agentRuntime.runEmbeddedAgent()`, it relies on `result.payloads` to extract spoken text via `extractSpokenTextFromPayloads()`. However, the embedded agent's post-turn compaction can complete *after* payloads are streamed, causing `result.payloads` to be empty or stale by the time TTS reads them. This means the voice call hears silence or truncated speech even though the agent produced a response.

## Summary

- Voice call TTS (text-to-speech) was receiving empty or stale payloads because `runEmbeddedAgent()` post-turn compaction could finish after the payloads were already streamed, and only `result.payloads` (the post-compaction aggregate) was consulted.
- This PR adds an `onBlockReply` callback (with `blockReplyBreak: "text_end"`) to `runEmbeddedAgent()` in `generateVoiceResponse()`, matching the same pattern already used by Telegram, Matrix, Copilot, and Codex channels to collect payloads before compaction completes. If `onBlockReply` payloads are available, they are preferred over `result.payloads`.
- Fixes #79521

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/voice-call` — 50 test files, 457 tests passed
- `pnpm build` — successful
- Autoreview: clean (2 non-blocking findings: P2 test gap for onBlockReply data collection path, P3 test type imprecision)
- CI: `check-test-types` fixed — added mock parameter type for `onBlockReply` in `createAgentRuntime`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Voice call TTS was receiving empty spoken text because `runEmbeddedAgent()` post-turn compaction could finish after streaming, making `result.payloads` empty when read.

Real environment tested: Linux (Node 22), branch `fix/issue-79521-voice-call-block-reply`, commit `d875bb8114`

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm test extensions/voice-call
pnpm build
pnpm tsgo:test
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ pnpm test extensions/voice-call

✓ extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts (10 tests) 49ms
  ✓ generateVoiceResponse (10 tests) 49ms
    ✓ suppresses reasoning payloads and reads structured spoken output
    ✓ extracts spoken text from fenced JSON
    ✓ returns silence for an explicit empty spoken contract response
    ✓ strips leading planning text when model returns plain text
    ✓ keeps plain conversational output when no JSON contract is followed
    ✓ pins the voice session to responseModel before running the embedded agent
    ✓ uses the persisted per-call session key for classic responses
    ✓ uses the main agent workspace when voice config omits agentId
    ✓ uses the configured voice response agent workspace
    ✓ passes the routed voice agent explicit tool allowlist to the embedded run

Test Files  50 passed (50)
     Tests  457 passed (457)
      Time  22.087s (in thread 4.271s, 517.38%)

$ pnpm build
> openclaw@2026.6.5 build /home/0668000452/codes/OPENSOURCES/openclaw
> turbo run build --filter='!docs' --filter='!@openclaw/apple-neural-engine'
...
Tasks: 79 successful, 79 total

$ pnpm tsgo:test
> pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
> node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json ...
> node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json ...
(no type errors — previously failing line 192 now passes)
```

Observed result after fix: All 10 voice-call response generator tests pass, including new assertions that `blockReplyBreak` is `"text_end"` and `onBlockReply` is a function. Type check (`tsgo:test`) now passes cleanly — the previously failing `extensions/voice-call/src/response-generator.test.ts:192` type errors (TS2493, TS2339) are resolved by adding parameter type annotation to the `runEmbeddedAgent` mock. Build completes successfully (79 tasks). The fix follows the established `blockReplyBreak`/`onBlockReply` contract already proven in Telegram, Matrix, Copilot, and Codex channels.

What was not tested: The `onBlockReply` data collection path is exercised only indirectly via the type assertion (`typeof ... onBlockReply` is `"function"`). A unit test that verifies payloads collected via `onBlockReply` are preferred over `result.payloads` is a follow-up item (tracked as P2 in review). No live Twilio/VoIP roundtrip was performed.

This PR changes subagent model resolution in reply runtime, stored model override inheritance, gateway session fallback, and related tests so spawned subagents prefer configured subagent defaults over parent models.

PR surface: Source +140, Tests +533. Total +673 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main lacks the reply-runtime subagent fallback and the PR body supplies a real macOS gateway/subagent run with persisted-model and provider-log evidence after the fix.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A narrow automated repair can preserve the existing no-plugin/lightweight normalization contract before maintainers decide the remaining compatibility and landing-order risks.

target.title: fix(subagent): resolve runtime model from subagent default instead of parent primary
target.body:
## Summary

Two-part fix for subagent model resolution:

- **Commit 1 — read-side fallback (defense in depth, fbbd3c2d5f):** `resolveSessionModelRef` had no subagent awareness. When `entry.model` was empty (race between the spawn-side write and the gateway-side read at run-start), it fell through to `resolveDefaultModelForAgent`, returning the parent agent's primary model instead of the configured subagent default. Also reorders the `??` chain in `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection` so global `agents.defaults.subagents.model` takes precedence over the spawning agent's own `model` field.
- **Commit 2 — Pi-harness write-side fix (production path, this commit):** `getReplyFromConfig` resolved the run model via `resolveDefaultModel` (which goes through `resolveDefaultModelForAgent` and is not subagent-aware), and post-run persistence in `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun` then wrote that model back to `entry.model`, silently flipping every subagent run onto the parent agent's primary. The new `resolveSubagentSessionDefaultModel` helper performs a one-time override after `sessionEntry` is loaded so the Pi runtime boots on the configured subagent default and the post-run write preserves it.

## Failure mode (before)

Config:
```
agents.list[main].model.primary = anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
agents.defaults.subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5
agents.list[main].subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5
```

Spawn flow:
1. Parent calls `sessions_spawn(task=..., agentId=main)` with no explicit `model:`.
2. Spawn-side computes the right ref (`gpt-5.5`) and writes `entry.model` / `entry.modelProvider` via `persistInitialChildSessionRuntimeModel`.
3. The Pi runtime starts the run via `getReplyFromConfig`, which resolves `defaultProvider/defaultModel` via `resolveDefaultModelForAgent` — returning Opus (parent primary) — and uses that as the run's `provider/model`.
4. Subagent runs on Opus instead of gpt-5.5.
5. Post-run, `updateSessionStoreAfterAgentRun → setSessionRuntimeModel` writes Opus back to `entry.model`, clobbering the spawn-time gpt-5.5.

Result on disk: `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/sessions.json` shows `model: "claude-opus-4-7"` for entries with `subagentRole: "orchestrator", spawnDepth: 1`, even though `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` correctly recorded `gpt-5.5`. The read-side fallback in commit 1 only kicks in while `entry.model` is empty — once the post-run write fires, `entry.model` is non-empty and the fallback is skipped.

## Fix

- **Commit 1 — `resolveSessionModelRef`:** when `entry.spawnDepth >= 1` or `entry.subagentRole` is set, fall back to the configured subagent default for this agent before falling through to the agent's primary.
- **Commit 1 — `resolveSubagentConfiguredModelSelection`:** reorder `??` chain to `agentConfig.subagents.model` → `agents.defaults.subagents.model` → `agentConfig.model`.
- **Commit 2 — `resolveSubagentSessionDefaultModel`:** new helper in `directive-handling.defaults.ts` that, given the loaded `sessionEntry`, checks the same subagent shape (`subagentRole` set or `spawnDepth >= 1`) and resolves the configured subagent default. `getReplyFromConfig` invokes it once after `sessionEntry` is loaded and overrides `defaultProvider/defaultModel/provider/model` so the Pi runtime boots and post-run-persists on the right model.

## Tests

- Read-side fallback tests in `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` cover the runtime subagent fallback (with and without `entry.spawnDepth`).
- Spawn-side chain-order tests cover the case where `agentConfig.subagents.model` is unset, `agents.defaults.subagents.model` is set, and `agentConfig.model` is set — we now return the global default instead of the agent's own primary.
- New tests in `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.subagent.test.ts` cover the Pi-harness write-side helper: subagent sessions resolve to the configured subagent default; non-subagent sessions return null so the parent's primary is preserved.
- Existing get-reply test mocks updated to expose the new export.

## Scope

Commit 1: `src/gateway/session-utils.ts`, `src/agents/model-selection.ts`, and their tests.
Commit 2: `src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts`, and one new test plus two existing test-mock updates.
No drive-by changes.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Spawned subagent runs persisted the parent agent's primary model (e.g. `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`) into `~/.openclaw/agents/<agent>/sessions/sessions.json` even though the configured subagent default was `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` and the spawn-side resolver had already written the correct ref. Subsequent turns then ran on the wrong model.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw install on macOS, Node 22, single-agent config with `agents.list[main].model.primary = anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `agents.defaults.subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, and `agents.list[main].subagents.model.primary = openai-codex/gpt-5.5`. Subagent spawned via `sessions_spawn(task=..., agentId=main)` from a parent Telegram chat with no explicit `model:` argument.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw doctor` to confirm config parses cleanly.
  2. Start gateway: `pnpm openclaw gateway start`.
  3. From the active chat, ask the agent to spawn a subagent with no model override.
  4. While the subagent is running and again after it completes, inspect `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` and `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Inspected `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` directly with `jq` — entries with `subagentRole: "orchestrator"` and `spawnDepth: 1` now persist `model: "gpt-5.5"` and `modelProvider: "openai-codex"` instead of `claude-opus-4-7`. `~/.openclaw/subagents/runs.json` continues to record `gpt-5.5` as before, and the gateway run trace prints the `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` provider/model pair when the run starts. Before the fix the same inspection printed `model: "claude-opus-4-7"` for those subagent entries even though the runs file recorded `gpt-5.5`.
- **Observed result after fix**: Subagent runs boot on `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` (visible in CLI run output and provider request logs), and the post-run write preserves that model in `sessions.json`, so subsequent turns of the same subagent session keep using `gpt-5.5`. Parent (non-subagent) sessions still resolve to `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` as expected.
- **What was not tested**: Cross-agent configurations where the agent has no `subagents.model` and only `agents.defaults.subagents.model` is set were exercised only via the new unit tests, not on a live install. None of the alias-resolution paths were exercised against a real OpenRouter alias index live.

### Post-rebase verification (2026-05-12)

Rebase merged `upstream/main` (`e7e3e903bf`) into the branch and addressed the clawsweeper P2 finding on `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts:404-416`. Specifically: a spawned subagent child entry with no own `providerOverride`/`modelOverride` could still inherit the parent session's persisted `/model` override via `resolveStoredModelOverride` (parent-inheritance branch), silently flipping the run back to the parent's model. `resolveStoredModelOverride` now skips the parent-inheritance branch when the child entry is a subagent session (`subagentRole` set or `spawnDepth >= 1`). Direct child overrides and non-subagent (topic/thread) child sessions are unaffected. New regression coverage in `src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.test.ts`.

Commands run:

- `pnpm tsgo` (core typecheck) -> exit 0, no diagnostics
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/stored-model-override.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/directive-handling.defaults.subagent.test.ts src/agents/model-selection.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-formatting.test.ts` -> 4 vitest shards passed (236 tests)
- `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply.block-streaming.test.ts` -> passed (1 test)
- `pnpm format:check` on touched files -> all clean after one auto-fix on `stored-model-override.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on touched files -> 0 warnings, 0 errors

target.title: fix(skills): clean up partially-copied skill dirs after sync failure
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes spurious "skipped missing skill file" warnings on every session bootstrap.

## Problem

`syncSkillsToWorkspace` copies skills from the agent's source workspace into the sandbox target workspace. When a copy fails (e.g. the source is a stale `.skill` zip artifact rather than a directory, or the source dir doesn't exist), the catch block logs a warning but leaves the destination in whatever state it reached. Subsequent snapshot builders then find a broken/incomplete skill directory and include it with an invalid `filePath`, causing `collectClaudePluginSkills` to emit a "skipped missing skill file" warning on every session bootstrap.

## Fix

After a failed copy, `await fsp.rm(dest, ...)` removes the partially-written destination. This ensures only successfully-synced skills are visible in the target workspace, so snapshot builders naturally exclude them.

## Root Cause (per Igor's analysis in AI-459)

1. `codex-subagent-worker` has a stale `.skill` zip in the default workspace's `skills/` dir
2. `matt-tasks` has no directory at all in the default workspace
3. `syncSkillsToWorkspace` tries to copy these, fails silently, and the snapshot still references the non-existent target paths
4. The Claude CLI plugin then logs a warn for each missing file per session bootstrap

Closes AI-459

target.title: fix: harden Mattermost slash callback auth
target.body:
## Fix Summary
Mattermost native slash commands were insecure in two ways: when `commands.callbackUrl` was omitted, OpenClaw auto-derived a plain-HTTP callback URL; and once commands were registered, any valid callback token for the account could be replayed against any slash trigger. This patch fails closed on insecure derived callback URLs and binds each registered callback token to its exact slash trigger before executing the command.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #65624

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.6 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 8.6 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `docs/channels/mattermost.md` (+4/-1)
- `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts` (+25/-11)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.ts` (+13/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts` (+2/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts` (+17/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.ts` (+24/-1)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-state.ts` (+12/-0)

### Technical Analysis
The slash registration path previously accepted a derived `http://...` callback when operators omitted `commands.callbackUrl`, which exposed reusable Mattermost command tokens in cleartext on the network path. Separately, the HTTP callback handler validated only that the presented token belonged to the account, then trusted the caller-supplied slash command name. The fix stops native slash registration unless operators provide an explicit HTTPS callback URL for non-derived deployments, and it tracks the exact registered trigger for each callback token so replaying a token against a different command returns `Unauthorized: invalid command token.`.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/slash-http.send-config.test.ts`
- Status: passed
- Command: `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor-slash.test.ts`
- Status: passed

## Risk and Compatibility
- behavior change for insecure setups: native slash command auto-registration now stops until operators configure an explicit reachable HTTPS `commands.callbackUrl`; valid callbacks keep working once configured

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/gpt-5.4

target.title: feat: cute GTK-native Linux App (#75)
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: - OpenClaw did not have an in-repo native Linux companion app, so Linux users were missing a first-class desktop surface for gateway lifecycle, diagnostics, tray/status behavior, onboarding, and key operational management flows. - The existing Linux direction in #75 called for a GTK-native companion rather than treating Linux as only a wrapper or dashboard handoff. - Why it matters: - Linux is a real target platform in the issue, and the lack of a native companion left users without parity on core desktop workflows such as service state visibility, tray/status integration, local diagnostics, and direct management of gateway-backed sections. - Without a native app, Linux-specific concerns such as systemd user-service integration, Ubuntu GNOME tray behavior, local-vs-remote resource access, and distro-native dependency flow were not handled in a coherent product surface. - What changed: - Added a new native Linux companion app under `apps/linux` using C, GTK4, and Libadwaita, plus a private GTK3 Ayatana/AppIndicator tray helper for Ubuntu GNOME tray/status integration. - Added a native gateway client stack for Linux: config loading, HTTP health probing, authenticated WebSocket connection, typed RPC mutation helpers, gateway data adapters, and controller-based UI sections. - Added native operational management surfaces for Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances, including config-safe editing, locality-aware transcript/log opening, pairing actions, and richer management flows previously absent on Linux. - Added Linux runtime/readiness modeling, onboarding, diagnostics, notifications, systemd integration, and focused automated tests covering parser behavior, mutation request shapes, RPC lifecycle handling, config rebuild safety, runtime/readiness logic, and helper behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): - This PR does not add distro packaging artifacts such as `.deb`, `.rpm`, or Snap packages. - This PR does not attempt to solve the Windows side. - This PR does not claim full macOS parity across every desktop capability; it establishes the native Linux companion foundation and a substantial in-app management surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [x] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [x] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #75 - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: - N/A - Missing detection / guardrail: - N/A - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - N/A - Why this regressed now: - N/A - If unknown, what was ruled out: - N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - N/A - Scenario the test should lock in: - N/A - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - N/A - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - N/A - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Linux now has a native GTK4 + Libadwaita companion app in-repo. - Ubuntu GNOME users get tray/status integration through the companion plus the private AppIndicator helper. - The Linux app can discover and work with the user `openclaw-gateway.service` lifecycle through native systemd integration. - The app surfaces first-run progression clearly across the main readiness states: - before `openclaw setup` - after `openclaw setup` but before gateway install - after gateway install with the service active and the gateway answering - The app surfaces runtime/readiness/onboarding/diagnostics states directly in the Linux UI. - Channels can now be inspected and managed in-app, including probe, logout, config editing, and WhatsApp QR linking flow. - Skills can now be filtered and managed in-app, including enable/disable, install, and env/API-key updates. - Sessions can now be managed in-app, including patch/reset/compact/delete and log access. - Cron can now be managed in-app, including scheduler status, recent runs, create/edit, run-now, enable/disable, delete, and transcript access. - Instances now expose richer remote node details, pending pairing approval/reject flows, and copy-debug-info behavior. - Log/transcript opening is locality-aware: local resources open locally when available, otherwise the UI falls back safely. - Channel config editing now validates edited JSON, preserves unrelated config keys, and saves using `baseHash`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user on Linux] -> [CLI / dashboard / partial prototype paths] -> [limited lifecycle visibility and little or no native management] After: [user on Linux] -> [native GTK4 companion app] -> [systemd + HTTP + authenticated WebSocket runtime model] -> [tray / diagnostics / onboarding / management sections] -> [native actions for channels, skills, sessions, cron, instances] ```` ## Security Impact (required) * New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: * The Linux companion introduces a new native desktop surface that can read local runtime context, inspect service state, connect to the local gateway over HTTP/WebSocket, edit config, open local logs/transcripts when available, and trigger gateway-backed management mutations. * Risk is mitigated by keeping the runtime model explicit, separating systemd discovery from gateway RPC transport, validating config JSON before save, preserving unrelated config content during rebuild, using `baseHash` on config writes, hardening request shapes through typed mutation helpers, and covering lifecycle/parsing/request-shape behavior in targeted tests. * Local-vs-remote resource access is handled explicitly so the app does not blindly assume every gateway-returned path is locally openable. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment * OS: * Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME * Runtime/container: * Bare-metal / local user-session workflow with systemd user service * Model/provider: * Local gateway-backed companion flow; model/provider depends on gateway config * Integration/channel (if any): * systemd user service, HTTP health, authenticated gateway WebSocket, GTK4 + Libadwaita, Ayatana/AppIndicator * Relevant config (redacted): * Fresh local OpenClaw config and user `openclaw-gateway.service`; secrets redacted ### Steps 1. Install Linux build dependencies and clone/build OpenClaw. 2. Build and run the Linux app with Meson. 3. Launch the Linux companion app before running `openclaw setup`. 4. Run `openclaw setup`, then relaunch or refresh the companion app. 5. Install and start the user gateway service. 6. Launch or refresh the Linux companion app again. 7. Exercise tray/status, diagnostics, onboarding/readiness, and the Dashboard / General / Channels / Skills / Sessions / Cron / Instances sections. 8. Run the Linux test suite. ### Expected * Before setup, the app reports the missing setup/runtime state clearly. * After setup but before gateway install, the app reports configured-but-not-installed service state clearly. * After gateway install/start, the app reports a healthy connected state, with diagnostics, dashboard, and general surfaces reflecting the running gateway. * The Linux app builds, launches, connects through the native runtime model, reflects systemd/gateway state accurately, and exposes native management flows for the major gateway-backed sections. * The Linux test suite passes. ### Actual * Matches expected in local verification. ## Evidence Attach at least one: * [x] Failing test/log before + passing after * [x] Trace/log snippets * [x] Screenshot/recording * [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What I personally verified (not just CI), and how: * Verified scenarios: * Built the Linux app from a clean checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 and Ubuntu 26.04 GNOME. * Ran the Meson test suite locally. * Verified tray helper spawn and tray/status behavior. * Verified systemd user-service discovery and lifecycle reflection. * Verified diagnostics/readiness/runtime presentation. * Verified first-run progression manually with screenshots: * app launched before `openclaw setup` * app after `openclaw setup` * app after gateway install/start with the gateway answering properly * Verified the healthy connected state manually against a fresh configured local gateway. * Verified additional Linux app surfaces manually, including Dashboard, General, and Diagnostics. * Verified the main Linux companion window and controller-backed sections load and refresh. * Verified native management flows across Channels, Skills, Sessions, Cron, and Instances at the implementation level. * Verified config editing flow behavior, including full-document rebuild and hash-aware save path. * Verified locality-aware log/transcript fallback behavior. * Edge cases checked: * Invalid config JSON aborts save. * Config rebuild preserves unrelated top-level keys and sibling channel config. * Mutation request shapes match the intended backend contract structure. * RPC lifecycle handling covers success/error/timeout/disconnect cleanup paths. * Tray-helper lifecycle and cleanup behavior were tightened. * Readiness states remain intelligible across the bootstrap sequence from no setup -> configured -> installed/running. * What I did **not** verify: * Official distro packaging artifacts (`.deb`, `.rpm`, Snap) are not included here. * Broad desktop-environment compatibility beyond the Ubuntu GNOME target path. * Full end-to-end parity for every macOS companion capability. ## Review Conversations * [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. * [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration * Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) * Yes * Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) * No * Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) * No * If yes, exact upgrade steps: * N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. * Risk: * Linux desktop behavior can vary by distro and shell, especially around tray hosting. * Mitigation: * Target the initial implementation clearly at Ubuntu GNOME, use a private AppIndicator helper, and keep the helper boundary isolated from the GTK4 main process. * Risk: * The Linux companion now depends on a larger native runtime stack: systemd context, HTTP health, WebSocket RPC, and gateway payload parsing. * Mitigation: * Keep those concerns separated into dedicated modules and cover parser/RPC/mutation behavior with focused tests. * Risk: * Config editing could accidentally overwrite unrelated config content if the save path is too narrow. * Mitigation: * Load the full config document, edit only the relevant subtree, rebuild the full document, validate JSON before save, and send `baseHash` with `config.set`. * Risk: * Transcript/log opening can fail or behave incorrectly if the gateway resource is not local to the GTK host. * Mitigation: * Enforce locality-aware fallback logic and only open local resources directly when they are actually present and accessible. * Risk: * This is a large first-class app addition and therefore a large review surface. * Mitigation: * Keep the app organized into focused subsystems and section controllers, provide a documented Linux build/test path, and include targeted tests for the most contract-sensitive areas.

target.title: fix(gateway): unblock startup and fresh agent sessions
target.body:
## Summary

- rebase the patch onto current `main`
- allow `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1` to defer pre-ready channel startup maintenance and startup session migration
- keep deferred failures visible by warning on rejected background startup tasks
- add regression coverage for fast mode and normal mode behavior

## Testing

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-plugins.test.ts`
- `env -u OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-runtime-config.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.secrets.test.ts`
- `pnpm test` *(repo-wide current-main failures still exist outside this diff; in this environment the first gateway auth failures were caused by a pre-set `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN`, and later unrelated failures were in untouched files including `src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts`, `src/config/io.compat.test.ts`, `src/plugins/bundled-plugin-metadata.test.ts`, and `extensions/opencode-go/index.test.ts`)*

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Allow `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1` to defer pre-ready channel startup maintenance and startup session migration without swallowing deferred failures, while preserving the existing blocking behavior in normal mode.

**Real environment tested**:
- local PR branch rebased onto current `origin/main`
- foreground OpenClaw dev gateway run from this branch on Linux
- gateway started with `--auth none --port 19041`

**Exact steps or command run after the patch**:
```bash
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1 node openclaw.mjs --dev gateway run --allow-unconfigured --auth none --port 19041 --verbose
curl --silent http://127.0.0.1:19041/health
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```text
15:43:23 [gateway] starting HTTP server...
15:43:24 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, device-pair, discord, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 9.2s)
15:43:27 [gateway] ready
```

```json
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

**Observed result after fix**:
- with `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_FAST_CONFIG=1`, the gateway reached `ready` and answered `/health` from a real foreground run on this branch
- fast mode no longer blocks readiness on startup maintenance/session migration before plugin loading completes
- the deferred failure path now warns instead of silently swallowing a rejected background startup task
- normal mode still waits for both startup tasks before plugin loading continues

**What was not tested**:
- a live forced failure of channel-maintenance or session-migration work during the foreground gateway run
- production-scale startup/session-migration workloads
- every channel plugin's startup-maintenance implementation

## AI assistance

- AI-assisted: GitHub Copilot CLI
- I reviewed the final patch and understand the changed startup path
- I resolved the applicable bot-review findings that were in scope for the final diff
- I attempted `codex review --base origin/main`, but local Codex auth in this environment was expired (`refresh_token_reused`), so there is no local Codex artifact to attach

target.title: fix(talk): resolve config from runtime secrets
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `talk.config` could pass raw `talk.providers.*.apiKey` SecretRef wrappers into provider-specific config resolution.
- Why it matters: Talk clients use `talk.config` for discovery, so a documented SecretRef-backed provider key could break discovery even though runtime Talk calls had the resolved secret.
- What changed: `talk.config` now resolves provider details from the active runtime config before redacting the response, while still returning the source config redacted.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no SecretRef schema, provider API, or client protocol changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #72496
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the `talk.config` read-scope response was using the source config provider block as the input to `resolveTalkConfig`, even when the active runtime config already contained resolved SecretRef values.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering SecretRef-shaped `talk.providers.*.apiKey` values in the `talk.config` discovery path.
- Contributing context (if known): `talk.speak` already used the runtime snapshot, so only the discovery/redaction path regressed.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `talk.config` redacts a source SecretRef while provider resolution receives the runtime-resolved string value.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it exercises the gateway handler seam without needing a live secrets backend or native Talk client.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts` covers redacted `talk.config` responses but not provider SecretRef resolution.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`talk.config` succeeds for documented SecretRef-backed `talk.providers.*.apiKey` config instead of failing discovery.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
talk.config -> source provider config -> resolveTalkConfig(unresolved SecretRef) -> error

After:
talk.config -> runtime provider config -> resolveTalkConfig(resolved secret) -> redacted response
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the handler uses the active runtime-resolved secret only as input to provider-specific config normalization, then returns the existing redacted response shape. The regression test asserts both the source SecretRef and resolved provider config are redacted.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: stub Talk provider
- Integration/channel (if any): Talk gateway RPC
- Relevant config (redacted): `talk.providers.acme.apiKey` as a SecretRef object in source config and resolved string in runtime config

### Steps

1. Configure `talk.providers.<id>.apiKey` as a SecretRef object.
2. Resolve runtime config through the active gateway snapshot.
3. Call `talk.config`.

### Expected

- `talk.config` returns the Talk provider snapshot with secret fields redacted.

### Actual

- Before this fix, provider config resolution could see the unresolved SecretRef object and fail discovery.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `CI=true OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=0 npm exec pnpm@10.33.0 -- test src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts src/gateway/server.talk-config.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked: source SecretRef remains redacted; provider-specific resolved config remains redacted; existing `talk.config` coverage still passes.
- What you did **not** verify: live iOS/macOS Talk client against a real ElevenLabs/OpenAI key.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: provider-specific config resolution could accidentally expose resolved secrets in the response.
  - Mitigation: keep the existing redaction pass on the returned payload and assert the response uses `REDACTED_SENTINEL` for both source and resolved config fields.

## Summary

- [ ] `sessions.revokeSharedToken` invalidates token immediately
- [ ] Tokens persist across gateway restarts via JSONL backing
- [ ] `pnpm check:changed` passes

Thanks @ioodu

The PR adds global inbound debounce flushing, followup/channel run queue drain waiters, SIGUSR1 restart integration, and channel teardown updates to avoid losing buffered inbound or followup messages during reload.

PR surface: Source +573, Tests +774. Total +1347 across 38 files.

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: [codex] fix: allow trusted .openclaw symlink
target.body:
## Summary

Closes #72572.

This restores support for dotfile-managed `~/.openclaw` directories while keeping exec approval paths hardened on both the Node and macOS node-host paths.

- allow trusted first-level `~/.openclaw` symlinks on POSIX after validating link ownership, link location, target ownership, target permissions, and stable resolved ancestors
- keep Windows `.openclaw` symlink targets blocked until ACL validation exists
- keep rejecting user-controlled chained symlink targets, deeper symlink traversal, and symlinked `exec-approvals.json` destinations
- fail closed for unsafe approvals reads in runtime policy paths while keeping snapshot/control-plane reads usable
- apply equivalent state-dir, JSON store, and socket-parent hardening on macOS, including symlinked `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` overrides and system `/tmp`/`/var` symlink roots
- document the supported symlink shape in approvals CLI docs

## Validation

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/exec-approvals.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`
- `swiftc -parse apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/ExecApprovals.swift apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/ExecApprovalsSocket.swift apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/NodeMode/MacNodeRuntime.swift apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/ExecApprovalsSocketPathGuardTests.swift apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/ExecApprovalsStoreRefactorTests.swift`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed` (core lanes passed; apps lane stopped because `swiftlint` is not installed locally)
- `swift test --filter ExecApprovalsSocketPathGuardTests` attempted; SwiftPM hung while downloading/building Sparkle artifact locally
- `codex review -c model="gpt-5.4" --base origin/main`

AI-assisted: yes

target.title: fix(cron): warn on main heartbeat handoff ghost runs
target.body:
Fixes #63106.

## Summary

- Add a scoped warning for fast successful main-session `systemEvent` cron jobs using `wakeMode="next-heartbeat"`.
- Persist the `possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run` annotation in cron run logs.
- Carry and render cron run-log warnings in the Control UI run history.
- Add `cron.ghostRunWarningThresholdMs` config with schema/help metadata and public docs.
- Keep `CronRunLogEntry.warnings` source-compatible in generated Swift by defaulting the optional initializer parameter to `nil`.
- Keep release-note context in the PR body only; this branch does not edit `CHANGELOG.md`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Verified that a fast successful main-session `systemEvent` cron handoff using `wakeMode: "next-heartbeat"` writes the `possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run` warning into the cron run log, that Control UI run history can render that warning from `CronRunLogEntry.warnings`, and that existing Swift call sites can construct `CronRunLogEntry` without passing the new optional `warnings` label.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout on current PR head `5238f67159`, rebased on current `upstream/main` `4a206db106`, using the real `buildGatewayCronService`, real cron run-log reader, Control UI cron run-history renderer, protocol generators, and Swift compiler typecheck. The cron proof used an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME` and did not touch the real user cron store.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rebased onto current `upstream/main`, regenerated gateway protocol artifacts, ran focused cron/gateway and Control UI tests, checked formatting/whitespace, verified generated protocol output stability, and typechecked a Swift compatibility snippet that constructs `CronRunLogEntry` without `warnings`.

Evidence after fix: Current-head focused test output:

```console
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts --reporter verbose

 Test Files  3 passed (3)
      Tests  42 passed (42)
   Start at  18:42:27
   Duration  16.44s
```

```console
$ ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check scripts/protocol-gen-swift.ts src/gateway/server-cron.ts src/gateway/server-cron.test.ts src/cron/run-log.ts src/cron/cron-protocol-conformance.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts
Checking formatting...

All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 250ms on 7 files using 12 threads.
```

Current-head isolated run-log proof shape, redacted from the real gateway cron service/run-log path:

```json
{
  "home": "/tmp/openclaw-ghost-proof.redacted",
  "jobId": "redacted-job-id",
  "logPath": "/tmp/openclaw-ghost-proof.redacted/runs/redacted-job-id.jsonl",
  "latest": {
    "action": "finished",
    "status": "ok",
    "summary": "proof handoff",
    "runId": "cron:redacted-job-id:redacted-start-ms",

    "deliveryStatus": "not-requested",

      "possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run"
    ]
  }
}
```

Swift compatibility proof:

```console
$ swiftc -typecheck \
  apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/AnyCodable.swift \
  apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift \
  /private/tmp/openclaw-pr72677-swift-compat.swift
(no output)
```

The compatibility snippet constructs `CronRunLogEntry(...)` without a `warnings:` argument, matching existing Swift call sites.

Protocol generation stability:

```console
$ node --import tsx scripts/protocol-gen.ts
wrote /Users/antonio/projects/openclaw_worktrees/pr72677_clean/dist/protocol.schema.json

$ node --import tsx scripts/protocol-gen-swift.ts
```

Format and whitespace checks:

Observed result after fix: The forced main-session `next-heartbeat` handoff completed successfully, the persisted run-log entry contains `warnings: ["possible-main-next-heartbeat-ghost-run"]`, Control UI run history renders that warning as a warning chip, regenerated Swift now emits `warnings: [String]? = nil`, and an old-style Swift initializer call without `warnings:` typechecks.

What was not tested: I did not dispatch a real scheduled agent turn from the user's production cron store; the warning proof used an isolated temporary OpenClaw home with the real gateway cron service and run-log modules so it would not affect real scheduled jobs. Full `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit` could not run on this machine because the active developer directory is Command Line Tools only and SwiftPM rejects the package platform declarations `.iOS(.v18)` / `.macOS(.v15)` before compiling sources; the targeted Swift compiler proof above covers the specific initializer compatibility blocker.

## Tests
