target.title: [codex] suppress heartbeat-isolated exec self-wake loop
target.body:
## Summary

Suppress node-level `exec` system-event replay for synthetic heartbeat-isolated sessions.

When a heartbeat run is already executing inside a synthetic `:heartbeat` session, any internal `exec.started` / `exec.finished` / `exec.denied` node event should stay in that transcript only. Re-enqueuing the same event as a system event and immediately calling `requestHeartbeatNow(...)` creates a self-wake loop:

```text
heartbeat -> exec -> exec-event wake -> heartbeat
```

This PR adds a narrow guard in `server-node-events` so entries tagged with `heartbeatIsolatedBaseSessionKey` do not enqueue another system event or request another heartbeat wake.

## Root cause

`server-node-events` currently canonicalizes the incoming session key and then treats every node `exec.*` event the same way:

- enqueue a system event on the canonical session key
- request an `exec-event` heartbeat wake

That is correct for normal agent sessions, but it is wrong for synthetic heartbeat-isolated sessions. Those sessions already contain the inline tool/exec transcript for the current run. Re-injecting the same exec lifecycle event through the system-event queue creates an unnecessary second heartbeat run against the same synthetic session.

The result is bursty extra heartbeat traffic and avoidable token spend, especially when a heartbeat falls back to small `exec` probes such as reading local state files.

## What changed

- In `src/gateway/server-node-events.ts`, load the full session entry alongside the canonical key.
- If the resolved entry has `heartbeatIsolatedBaseSessionKey`, return early for node `exec.started` / `exec.finished` / `exec.denied` handling.
- Add a focused regression test proving `exec.finished` on `agent:main:main:heartbeat` does **not** enqueue a system event or request another heartbeat wake.

## Scope boundary

This PR is intentionally narrow.

It does **not** change:

- normal node-exec notifications for non-heartbeat sessions
- isolated-heartbeat prompt construction
- isolated-heartbeat persona/bootstrap behavior
- wake coalescing or scheduler policy

It only suppresses the node-event feedback path for synthetic heartbeat sessions that already have the exec/tool transcript inline.

## Why this is distinct from nearby heartbeat fixes

This sits in the same bug family as recent isolated-heartbeat fixes, but it covers a different failure mode:

- `#59606` stabilizes repeated `:heartbeat` suffix growth after wake re-entry.
- `#67059` fixes isolated heartbeat exec prompts/persona once an exec event is already being relayed.

This PR prevents one specific class of redundant relays from being emitted in the first place.

## Validation

Ran on the PR branch:

- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/gateway/server-node-events.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsc -p tsconfig.test.src.json --noEmit`
- `pnpm check:changed --staged`

`check:changed --staged` completed successfully for this diff, including the gateway-targeted changed test plan.

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): stop forwarding embedding dimensions upstream
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `memory-lancedb` forwarded `dimensions` to every direct OpenAI-compatible embeddings request, which breaks vLLM/NVIDIA-style providers that reject that field. - Why it matters: memory recall/search can fail at embed time for otherwise compatible providers. - What changed: the direct embeddings client now preserves server-side `dimensions` first, then retries without `dimensions` only when the provider clearly rejects that field; the fallback vector is truncated locally to the configured dimension. - Scope boundary: config shape, provider selection, secrets handling, runtime permissions, and migrations are unchanged. ## Change Type - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope - [x] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes none - Related none - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause - Root cause: the direct embedding wrapper assumed every OpenAI-compatible provider would accept `dimensions`, then passed it straight through upstream. - Missing guardrail: no regression test covered a provider that rejects `dimensions`, or the compatibility requirement that accepting providers still receive server-side `dimensions`. ## Regression Test Plan - Coverage level: Unit test - Target test file: `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts` - Scenarios locked in: - accepting OpenAI-compatible providers receive `dimensions` upstream; - providers that clearly reject the `dimensions` field are retried without it and use local truncation; - unsupported dimension values stay unavailable without a fallback retry; - short fallback vectors return the memory-recall unavailable result instead of rejecting the tool call. - Supplemental local run: ```text $ .\node_modules\.bin\vitest.CMD run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts -t "sends OpenAI embedding dimensions upstream when accepted|retries without OpenAI embedding dimensions and truncates locally when rejected|returns unavailable when fallback embedding is shorter than the configured dimensions|does not retry without dimensions when the provider rejects the dimension value" Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 4 passed | 124 skipped (128) ``` ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Memory recall still uses server-side dimensions for providers that accept them. Providers that reject `dimensions` now work through a retry without that field, as long as they return a vector long enough to truncate locally. ## Diagram ```text Before: memory_recall -> OpenAI-compatible request with dimensions -> provider rejects body.dimensions -> recall unavailable After, accepting provider: memory_recall -> request with dimensions -> provider-sized vector -> store/search After, rejecting provider: memory_recall -> request with dimensions -> provider rejects body.dimensions -> retry without dimensions -> receive full embedding -> local truncation -> store/search ``` ## Security Impact - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows - Runtime/container: local workspace with pnpm - Model/provider: `nvidia/nv-embed-v1` - Integration/channel: `memory-lancedb` plugin through real OpenClaw `ltm search` - Relevant config (redacted): `embedding.baseUrl=https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`, `embedding.model=nvidia/nv-embed-v1`, `embedding.dimensions=4` ### Steps 1. Configured `memory-lancedb` with NVIDIA's OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint and `embedding.dimensions=4`. 2. Initialized an isolated LanceDB database through the real OpenClaw plugin CLI. 3. Ran `openclaw ltm search "openclaw memory proof" --limit 1` against current main. 4. Ran the same command with the fixed branch plugin loaded. 5. Seeded the branch LanceDB database with a 4-dimensional proof vector and reran the branch search. ### Expected 1. Current main fails because it forwards `dimensions` with no fallback. 2. The fixed branch preserves server-side `dimensions` for providers that accept them. 3. For NVIDIA, the fixed branch retries without `dimensions` after the provider rejection and truncates the returned 4096-dimensional embedding locally to 4 dimensions. 4. LanceDB search succeeds with the configured 4-dimensional table. ### Actual - Current main failed with NVIDIA's live provider error: `400 ... Extra inputs are not permitted ... ('body', 'dimensions')`. - The fixed branch succeeded and returned the seeded LanceDB proof row: `proof branch locally truncated NVIDIA embedding dimensions`. - This confirms the compatibility fallback omits `dimensions` after the provider rejects them and uses local truncation before LanceDB search. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Perf numbers Current in main: After in this branch, empty DB: After in this branch, seeded DB: Earlier targeted regression test screenshot: ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: `memory-lancedb` OpenAI-compatible embeddings preserve server-side `dimensions` for providers that accept that field, while providers that reject `dimensions` can still recall/search memories through a retry without `dimensions` and local truncation of the fallback embedding. - Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw source checkout, pnpm workspace, real `memory-lancedb` plugin with LanceDB storage, NVIDIA OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint using a redacted API key, `embedding.baseUrl=https://integrate.api.nvidia.com/v1`, `embedding.model=nvidia/nv-embed-v1`, and `embedding.dimensions=4`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Configured an isolated OpenClaw runtime with the PR branch `memory-lancedb` plugin override, isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`. 2. Ran `node --import tsx src/entry.ts --no-color ltm search "openclaw memory proof" --limit 1` against the fixed branch and the real NVIDIA OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint. 3. Seeded the branch LanceDB database with a 4-dimensional proof memory row and reran the same `ltm search` command. 4. Compared with current `main`, which ran the same OpenClaw/LanceDB/NVIDIA setup and failed when `dimensions` was forwarded upstream without a fallback. - Evidence after fix: The terminal screenshots embedded above in the PR Evidence section show real OpenClaw output from the branch. The empty branch run returned `[]`, and the seeded branch run returned the LanceDB proof row with `id: proof-branch-local-truncation`, `text: proof branch locally truncated NVIDIA embedding dimensions`, and `score: 1`. The current-main screenshot shows the live provider rejection: `Extra inputs are not permitted` for `body.dimensions`. - Observed result after fix: The branch OpenClaw run completed memory search successfully against LanceDB after the NVIDIA-compatible provider rejected `dimensions`, the fallback request omitted `dimensions`, and the longer embedding was truncated locally to the configured 4-dimensional table. - What was not tested: No provider selection, secrets handling, runtime permissions, migration, or config-shape changes were tested because this PR does not change those surfaces. ## Human Verification - Verified scenarios: live OpenClaw memory-lancedb search against NVIDIA's OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint with `embedding.dimensions=4`; current main fails when `dimensions` is forwarded upstream without a fallback; the fixed branch succeeds by retrying without `dimensions` and truncating locally before LanceDB search. - Edge cases checked: provider rejecting upstream `dimensions`; configured 4-dimensional LanceDB table receiving a locally truncated vector from a longer provider embedding. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? No - Migration needed? No - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: a provider may reject `dimensions` and then return an embedding shorter than the configured local dimension. - Mitigation: the code returns the existing unavailable memory result with a clear error instead of storing or searching with a malformed vector.

target.title: feat(messages): add responseFooter and enrich /usage full
target.body:
## Summary

_AI-assisted PR. Prepared with AI assistance against a repo snapshot; targeted validation only. See Repro + Verification and Human Verification for exact coverage._

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: OpenClaw had a native `messages.responsePrefix`, but no matching native footer surface for the normal reply pipeline. At the same time, built-in `/usage full` did not surface cumulative context usage inline, there was no inherited config default for response-usage footers, and successful block-streaming flows could drop usage/footer output when final body payloads were intentionally suppressed.
- Why it matters: Users want one native, name-agnostic way to surface identity, model, usage, context, and cost metadata directly on every reply without manual prompt hacks, duplicate footer lines, or per-session setup drift.
- What changed: This PR adds `messages.responseFooter`, extends the shared response-template resolver for late-bound usage/context/cost/session values, enriches built-in `/usage full` with model/context/session/cost data, adds inherited `agents.defaults.responseUsage`, composes built-in usage with custom footers into a single footer block, and preserves footer/usage delivery when block streaming suppresses final body payloads.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not add a new `/usage context` mode, does not change message-tool outbound sends or other non-reply egress paths, and does not introduce generic free-form interpolation of arbitrary session fields; supported placeholders remain an explicit allowlist.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #40215
- Closes #12578
- Related #24014
- Related #17832
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

Note: `#24014` and `#17832` are currently closed upstream, so they are referenced as related context even though this PR implements the requested behavior in those threads. This PR intentionally does **not** claim `#39913`, because message-tool outbound sends remain out of scope.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: Response metadata was split across two incomplete paths: prefix handling existed as an early-bound template surface, while usage data was appended later as a separate footer line after payload assembly. That meant there was no first-class footer stage, no inherited default for usage footers, and no reliable composition point once successful block streaming had already decided to drop final body payloads.
- Missing detection / guardrail: There was no seam coverage for “block streaming succeeded and final payloads are empty but usage/footer still needs to be delivered,” and no explicit composition rule for combining built-in `/usage` output with a configured custom footer.
- Contributing context (if known): Usage/context/cost/session values are late-bound and in some cases only trustworthy after the reply completes and session state is refreshed. Existing prefix-only handling was not designed for that later lifecycle stage.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-usage-line.footer.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-session-usage.test.ts`
  - `src/plugin-sdk/channel-reply-pipeline.test.ts`
  - `src/config/runtime-overrides.test.ts`
  - `src/config/zod-schema.agent-defaults.test.ts`
  - `src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `/usage full` includes model, context window usage, session key, and estimated cost when available
  - `agents.defaults.responseUsage` is inherited by sessions that do not override it
  - explicit `responseUsage: "off"` remains explicit even when a global default exists, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` clears the override and re-enables inheritance
  - static `messages.responseFooter` values fold with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line to avoid duplicate footer lines
  - successful block streaming still returns a trailing footer/usage payload when final body payloads are intentionally suppressed
  - response prefixes do not leak raw late-bound `{cost}` / `{usageLine}` / context placeholders before those values are known
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The risk is in formatting, inheritance, and reply-payload composition. Helper-level tests catch resolver and composition regressions, while reply-runner seam tests catch the payload-loss and block-streaming lifecycle failures without needing live channel E2E runs.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing prefix-template coverage already exercised the shared resolver pattern; this PR extends that surface and adds footer- and usage-specific cases.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- New optional `messages.responseFooter` config for the normal reply pipeline.
- `messages.responsePrefix` and `messages.responseFooter` now share the same case-insensitive, allowlisted template resolver for placeholders such as `{model}`, `{provider}`, `{identityName}`, `{effort}`, `{input}`, `{output}`, `{total}`, `{cacheRead}`, `{cacheWrite}`, `{context}`, `{contextMax}`, `{contextPercent}`, `{cost}`, `{usageLine}`, and `{session}`.
- Built-in `/usage full` is enriched to include cumulative context usage, model label, session key, and estimated cost when available.
- New inherited config: `agents.defaults.responseUsage` (`off` | `tokens` | `full`). Sessions without an explicit override inherit this default.
- Session-level explicit `responseUsage: "off"` is preserved even when a global default exists. `PATCH responseUsage: null` still clears the explicit override and returns the session to inherited behavior.
- When both `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` are enabled:
  - static footers compose with built-in usage into one appended footer block
  - usage-aware custom footers suppress the separate built-in usage line
  - cost-only custom footers still compose with built-in usage unless `{usageLine}` is explicitly included
- In successful block-streaming flows, usage/footer text can still be emitted as a trailing payload even when the final body payload is intentionally suppressed.
- For streamed prefixes, unresolved late-bound usage/context/cost placeholders are blanked instead of leaking raw `{cost}`-style tokens.
- Added `docs/response-footer.md` documenting config, placeholders, composition rules, and examples.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [optional responsePrefix applied]
  -> [optional /usage line appended late]
  -> [block streaming may suppress final payloads]
  -> [custom footer unavailable natively, or usage/footer can be lost or duplicated]

After:
[user message]
  -> [reply generated]
  -> [early-bound prefix resolved]
  -> [late-bound usage/context/cost/session template context resolved]
  -> [built-in usage line formatted]
  -> [responseFooter composed with or against usage]
  -> [footer block appended to last text payload or emitted as trailing payload]
  -> [reply sent without duplicate footer lines]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: Node/TypeScript repo snapshot in an offline CAAS container
- Model/provider: mocked reply-runner and gateway paths with synthetic provider/model/usage metadata
- Integration/channel (if any): normal reply pipeline, successful block-streaming reply pipeline, gateway session patch path, and TUI `/usage` toggle path; no live external channel in this container
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "responseUsage": "full"
    },
    "list": [{ "id": "main", "identity": { "name": "Jarvis" } }]
  },
  "messages": {
    "responsePrefix": "*{identityName}:* {cost}",
    "responseFooter": "{usageLine}\n— {identityName}"
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure `agents.defaults.responseUsage` and optionally configure `messages.responseFooter`.
2. Trigger a normal reply, a successful block-streaming reply, and one or more `/usage` mode changes (`off`, `tokens`, `full`).
3. Inspect the final reply payloads and the effective session `responseUsage` state.

### Expected

- Sessions without an explicit override inherit `agents.defaults.responseUsage`.
- `/usage full` includes per-message tokens plus cumulative context, model, session, and estimated cost when available.
- Static custom footers fold with built-in usage into one footer block, while usage-aware footers suppress the separate built-in usage line.
- Successful block streaming does not drop footer/usage output just because the final body payload was suppressed.
- Prefixes do not leak raw late-bound placeholders before values are known.

### Actual

- In the covered paths, inherited usage defaults, explicit `off`, and clear-to-inherit (`null`) semantics behave as expected.
- `/usage full` is enriched with context/model/session/cost data in the covered reply paths.
- Footer composition follows the intended static-vs-usage-aware rules, and successful block streaming still returns footer/usage content as a trailing payload.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Representative snippets from the targeted coverage for this branch:

```text
Enriched built-in /usage full:
  Usage: 12k in / 450 out · cache 2.0k cached / 300 new · ctx 45k/200k (23%) · model gpt-5.4 · session `agent:main:webchat:dm:123`
```

```text
Static footer composed with built-in usage:
  ok

  Usage: 12 in / 3 out
  — footer
```

```text
Usage-aware footer suppressing duplicate built-in usage:
  ok

  ↑12 ↓3
```

```text
Successful block streaming with final body suppressed:
  streamed chunk: Hello
  trailing payload returned: Usage: 12 in / 3 out · cache 4 cached / 2 new
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the touched config/type/schema/help/label surfaces for `messages.responseFooter` and `agents.defaults.responseUsage`
  - reviewed the reply-runner composition path for normal replies and the success path where block streaming suppresses final body payloads
  - inspected the new resolver/composition tests for footer, usage, inheritance, and prefix late-binding behavior
  - ran `git diff --check`
  - parsed changed TypeScript files successfully with the TypeScript compiler API in the offline container
  - manually exercised targeted helper/runtime behavior for prefix soft-blanking, cost-only footer composition, and enriched full usage-line formatting
- Edge cases checked:
  - explicit `off` versus inherited default
  - `PATCH responseUsage: null` re-enables inheritance
  - static footer versus usage-aware footer versus cost-only footer composition
  - blank or whitespace-only footer templates emit nothing
  - unresolved footer placeholders remain literal, while unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders are blanked
  - successful block streaming can return only a trailing footer payload without prepending a verbose new-session notice
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
  - live Telegram/Discord/WhatsApp channel behavior
  - message-tool outbound send behavior (`#39913` remains intentionally out of scope)
  - screenshot-based verification in a running OpenClaw instance

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No review conversations exist yet because this PR body is being prepared before the PR is opened. Once the PR is live, the author should update these checkboxes based on the actual review state.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk:
  - Enabling both built-in `/usage` and `messages.responseFooter` could produce confusing duplicate footer lines.
  - Mitigation: Added explicit composition rules and tests that suppress the separate built-in usage line when the footer already consumes usage-aware placeholders.
- Risk:
  - Late-bound usage/context/cost values could be missing or stale when templates render.
  - Mitigation: The footer path resolves against late-bound reply/session data, prefers refreshed persisted session state when available, and soft-blanks unresolved late-bound prefix placeholders instead of leaking raw tokens.
- Risk:
  - Block-streaming channels may render the usage/footer as a separate trailing message rather than mutating the already-streamed body.
  - Mitigation: The implementation preserves delivery without trying to retroactively rewrite the streamed content, and dedicated reply-runner coverage locks in the suppressed-final-payload success path.
- Risk:
  - Inherited default semantics could confuse users if explicit `off` is not distinguishable from “unset.”
  - Mitigation: Explicit `off` is preserved in session state and TUI patching, while `PATCH responseUsage: null` remains the clear-to-inherit path; both behaviors are covered by tests and docs.

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: [codex] fix(telegram): allow ACP bindings for direct chats
target.body:
## Summary

This change allows top-level Telegram `bindings[]` entries with `type: "acp"` to target direct chats in addition to forum topics.

## Root Cause

Telegram ACP binding normalization and matching only accepted topic-shaped conversation ids. Plain direct-chat ids were rejected during configured-binding compilation, so direct-message ACP bindings never became eligible for route resolution and fell back to the default route/session path.

## Changes

- accept plain positive Telegram chat ids when compiling configured ACP bindings
- match direct-chat inbound conversations before topic parsing so DM bindings do not get misclassified as topic ids
- keep bare negative chat ids rejected so whole-group bindings are still not implicitly enabled
- extract the Telegram bindings adapter for focused unit coverage
- document that Telegram ACP bindings support direct chats as well as forum topics

## Validation

- `node --no-maglev /tmp/openclaw-upstream.hKdV5W/node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/telegram/src/channel.bindings.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.acp-bindings.test.ts`

target.title: Harden subagent completion delivery
target.body:
## Summary
- persist a per-run delivery claim and user-safe delivery payload for subagent completions
- route active-parent completion announces queue-first with fail-closed semantics instead of direct-first fallback behavior
- harden iMessage delivery normalization and chunking to avoid leaking internal context and to preserve multiline/code payloads safely

## Why
A production bug leaked internal subagent/control-plane envelopes into iMessage, and a second bug allowed duplicate user-visible completion delivery when a parent session was active while a child completion also delivered directly.

This change moves the fix to source-level architecture instead of relying on downstream `dist` patching:
- separates persisted user-delivery payloads from internal orchestration text
- makes announce delivery idempotent across queue/direct transitions with a persisted run claim
- prevents direct external delivery while an active parent session can still accept the completion through its announce queue

## Implementation Notes
- add `deliveryClaim`, `userDeliveryPayload`, and `fallbackUserDeliveryPayload` to subagent run records
- extract user-facing completion payloads from structured child-result blocks first, then named delivery sections, then sanitized fallback cleanup
- persist payload/claim state through completion freeze, cleanup retries, and steer restarts
- normalize iMessage text before send, close dangling code fences, and chunk around non-code boundaries when possible

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/subagent-announce-dispatch.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.format.e2e.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce-delivery.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run extensions/imessage/src/monitor/sanitize-outbound.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/deliver.test.ts`

When adding a second Feishu IM channel in the same OpenClaw instance,

making it impossible to register multiple independent Feishu bots.

## Changes

  existing Feishu accountIds.

## Backward Compatibility

- First Feishu account still defaults to `default` automatically.
- Existing configurations are not affected.

target.title: plugins: clarify allowlist warning when entries don't match discovered ids
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: When users set `plugins.allow` to a channel-style id (for example `["feishu"]`) instead of the real plugin id (`openclaw-lark`), the loader still emits `plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: ...` even though the allowlist is not empty — the message is literally incorrect and does not hint at the plugin-id vs. channel-id mismatch.
- Why it matters: Users see a contradictory warning (they configured the allowlist, but the log says it is empty) and have no direct clue that their entry does not correspond to any real plugin id, so they cannot fix the configuration without reading plugin manifests or source code.
- What changed: `warnWhenAllowlistIsOpen` now differentiates two cases: (1) `plugins.allow` is empty ⇒ existing warning wording is preserved, (2) `plugins.allow` has entries but none match any discovered plugin id ⇒ a new, specific warning listing the unknown entries alongside the discovered plugin ids, telling users to use the plugin id rather than a channel id or npm package name. When at least one allow entry matches a discovered id, the loader stays silent as before.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Allow/deny policy semantics, provenance/untracked-local warnings, auto-discovery rules, and all other loader behaviors are unchanged. No core allow/deny enforcement logic was moved.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68352
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `warnWhenAllowlistIsOpen` in `src/plugins/loader.ts` returned early whenever `params.allow.length > 0`, suppressing the warning entirely in the populated-but-mismatched case. When `params.allow.length === 0`, the warning fired with fixed text `plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: ...`, which was the only code path a user could hit — so populated-but-mismatched allowlists silently acted like an empty allowlist from the warning perspective.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No comparison between `params.allow` and the discovered plugin ids was ever performed inside the warning helper, so the loader could not distinguish the two failure modes.
- Contributing context (if known): Channel plugins like Feishu publish a plugin id (`openclaw-lark`) that does not line up with the channel config key (`channels.feishu`) or the npm package name (`feishu-openclaw-plugin`), which makes the mismatch easy to hit in practice.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/loader.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - `warns when plugins.allow entries do not match any discovered plugin ids`: asserts the new mismatch warning fires exactly once with the configured entries in quotes, the discovered plugin id, and the `Use the plugin id` hint, and does not emit the legacy `plugins.allow is empty` string.
  - `stays quiet when plugins.allow contains at least one matching plugin id`: asserts that a partial match (one real id plus a stray channel-style entry) suppresses both the empty-allowlist warning and the new mismatch warning, preserving current behavior for correctly-configured allowlists.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The loader-level unit tests exercise the warning helper through the real `loadOpenClawPlugins` entry point with a workspace-discovered plugin, matching the production call site at `warnWhenAllowlistIsOpen({ ...allow: normalized.allow ... })`.
- Existing test that already covers this: `warns about open allowlists only for auto-discovered plugins` covers the empty-allow case; left intact so we keep wording stability for the existing scenario.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu via WSL2 per original report, reproducible on any platform)
- Runtime/container: Node.js gateway, bundled + workspace plugin discovery
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Any external channel plugin whose plugin id differs from the channel config key (`feishu-openclaw-plugin` → plugin id `openclaw-lark`)
- Relevant config (redacted): `plugins.enabled = true`, `plugins.allow = ["feishu"]`, a non-bundled plugin whose real id is `openclaw-lark` is discoverable in the workspace.

### Steps

1. Install a non-bundled plugin whose id differs from its channel key (for example `openclaw-lark` published in `~/.openclaw/extensions/feishu-openclaw-plugin/`).
2. Start the gateway with `plugins.allow = ["feishu"]` in config.
3. Observe the `[plugins] ...` warning in startup logs.

### Expected

- A specific warning pointing at the mismatch, telling the user to use the plugin id and not the channel id or package name.

### Actual (before)

- `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: openclaw-lark (...). Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` — literally false because `plugins.allow` is populated, and unhelpful because it does not hint at the plugin-id vs. channel-id mismatch.

### Actual (after)

- `[plugins] plugins.allow entries "feishu" do not match any discovered plugin ids; discovered non-bundled plugins: openclaw-lark (...). Use the plugin id (not a channel id or npm package name).`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new test cases assert both the populated-but-mismatched and partial-match scenarios)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Ran the three relevant scoped tests (existing empty-allow case, new mismatched-allow case, new partial-match case) — all pass.
  - Ran the full `src/plugins/loader.test.ts` suite (95 tests) after the change — all pass.
  - `npx oxlint src/plugins/loader.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts` → 0 warnings / 0 errors.
  - `npx oxfmt --check src/plugins/loader.ts src/plugins/loader.test.ts CHANGELOG.md` → all files properly formatted.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Empty allowlist still produces the original warning text so downstream log/doc matchers continue to work.
  - Partial match (one real id + one unknown entry) stays silent, matching prior behavior for correctly-configured allowlists.
  - `emitWarning: false` callers (for example the CLI metadata path at loader.ts ~2355) remain silent.
- What you did **not** verify: `pnpm build` and repo-wide `pnpm check` have pre-existing failures on `upstream/main` in unrelated files (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin*`, `extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/aimock/server.ts`, `extensions/lobster/src/lobster-runner.ts`); the diff here does not touch those files, so the failures are not introduced by this PR.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — empty-allow warning text preserved; new mismatch path replaces what used to be silence.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users with noisy populated-but-mismatched allowlists will now see a startup warning where previously they saw none.
  - Mitigation: The new warning is more accurate and actionable (it names the unknown entries and the discovered plugin ids); the loader caches it per `warningCacheKey` so it fires at most once per registry load, matching the existing empty-allow warning cadence.

---

## Real behavior proof

Refreshed onto current `main` (2026-06-17) and verified the new warning fires from a **real run** of the built loader — not a unit test. A workspace plugin was seeded on disk (`<ws>/.openclaw/extensions/demo-workspace-plugin/` with `index.cjs` + `openclaw.plugin.json`), `plugins.allow` was set to a non-matching id (`["feishu"]`), and the **compiled** `loadOpenClawPlugins` (from `dist/`, post-`pnpm build`) was executed against it with bundled plugins disabled for clean output.

Captured verbatim from the real process (stderr):

```
[plugins] plugins.allow entries "feishu" do not match any discovered plugin ids; discovered non-bundled plugins: demo-workspace-plugin (/tmp/.../ws/.openclaw/extensions/demo-workspace-plugin/index.cjs). Use the plugin id (not a channel id or npm package name).
```

Negative controls on the same real loader confirm the warning is scoped correctly:
- `plugins.allow: ["demo-workspace-plugin"]` (matching the discovered id) → **no warning** (allow-match suppression works).
- non-activating snapshot load (`emitWarning=false`, the path `openclaw plugins list` uses) → **no warning**.

This isolates the new message to exactly the activating-load + populated-but-mismatched-allowlist case the PR introduces. (Induction note: the warning only emits on the activating load path — `warnWhenAllowlistIsOpen({ emitWarning: shouldActivate })` in `src/plugins/loader.ts` — so it surfaces during real plugin activation / gateway startup, not via `plugins list`, which intentionally passes `emitWarning: false`.)

Branch refreshed against `main`; the `CHANGELOG.md` entry was dropped (changelog is release-generated per `AGENTS.md`).

target.title: Fix delivery-ready verification blockers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR closes the remaining delivery-ready verification blockers for the current branch.

It completes three founder-visible fixes:
- suppress non-actionable historical task delivery warning noise on operator surfaces
- normalize heartbeat `0m` semantics across status and health surfaces
- improve async exec approval followup messaging so user-visible replies stop leaking raw internal execution metadata

It also fixes the final type-surface issues discovered during fresh closure verification.

## What changed

### Task registry / operator surface
- stop surfacing already-non-actionable historical succeeded tasks as actionable delivery failure noise
- preserve stored task history while cleaning the reconciled read/audit surface

### Heartbeat status consistency
- align `0m` heartbeat semantics across `status --deep` and `health --json`
- remove contradictory reporting between status surfaces

### Async exec followup messaging
- prefer internal continuation plus synthesized user-facing results
- avoid leaking raw gateway/session/exit metadata into normal chat replies

### Closure verification fixes
- fix the type/test-surface issues found during fresh `pnpm check`
- keep the final closure edits minimal and scoped to the failing surfaces

## Verification

Fresh verification on this branch:
- `pnpm lint` ✅
- `pnpm check` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Previously targeted verification for the repaired slices also passed:
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/tasks/task-registry.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/commands/health.command.coverage.test.ts src/commands/status-all/diagnosis.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts test/release-check.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-postpublish-verify.test.ts` ✅

## Notes

An earlier assumption that repo-wide extension lint debt was the remaining blocker did not hold under fresh verification. The real final blockers were a small set of type-surface failures, which are fixed here.

target.title: fix(config): allow historyLimit: 0 in GroupChatSchema
target.body:
## Summary

`GroupChatSchema.historyLimit` used `.positive()`, which rejects `0`, while the other `historyLimit` schemas accept `0` with `.min(0)`. This made `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` invalid even though `0` is the existing config value for disabling history.

Fixes #65305

## Fix

- Change `messages.groupChat.historyLimit` from `.positive()` to `.min(0)` in `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts`.
- Add focused runtime config schema tests for `0`, positive values, and negative values.
- Add a Gateway `config.patch` regression test proving the runtime API path accepts `historyLimit: 0`.

## Current Status

- Rebased cleanly on current `upstream/main` (`33b81686ad`).
- Current PR head is `a4f99c4cd2c05c26e52a06b315a5b81da24f509b`.
- Diff against current main is one commit: `git rev-list --left-right --count upstream/main...refresh-historylimit-zero` -> `0 1`.
- Main has not already fixed this: current main still has `.positive()` for `GroupChatSchema.historyLimit`.

## Test Plan

Current head verification after the final rebase:

- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts`
- [x] `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts -t "historyLimit: 0"`
- [x] `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check src/config/zod-schema.core.ts src/config/zod-schema.core.test.ts src/gateway/server.config-patch.test.ts`
- [x] `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `messages.groupChat.historyLimit: 0` must be accepted as a valid runtime config value so group chat history can be disabled.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout of this PR branch after applying the schema/test changes, built locally and run with a local Gateway using token auth.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built the branch with `OPENCLAW_RUN_NODE_SKIP_DTS_BUILD=1 pnpm build`, started the built local Gateway, connected through the backend Gateway protocol with `operator.admin`, read the runtime config schema, called `config.patch`, and read the config back from the running server.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local Gateway API smoke run:

```text
helloAuth.scopes: ["operator.admin"]
schema.messages.groupChat.historyLimit: { type: "integer", minimum: 0 }
before: { valid: true, historyLimit: 1 }
config.patch: { ok: true, noop: false, historyLimit: 0 }
after: { valid: true, historyLimit: 0 }
```

**Observed result after fix:** The running Gateway advertised `minimum: 0` for `messages.groupChat.historyLimit`; `config.patch` accepted `historyLimit: 0` with `ok: true`; a follow-up read returned `valid: true` and `historyLimit: 0`.

**What was not tested:** The dashboard browser clickpath was not exercised; this proof targets the runtime Gateway/API config validation path directly.

This specifically covers the concern that direct config loading may accept a value while runtime JSON Schema/API validation may still reject it.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: test(gateway): consolidate deriveSessionTitle tests into dedicated module
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes an issue where heartbeat polls could overwrite explicit user-labeled session titles with transcript-derived titles like `"heartbeat"`.

## Changes

- Extracts `deriveSessionTitle` to `session-title.ts` with a dedicated test module.
- Preserves explicit session labels before transcript-derived fallbacks.
- Keeps auto-populated `origin.label` behind `firstUserMessage` so unlabeled inbound DMs can still use transcript-derived titles.
- Keeps `session-utils.ts` as a compatibility re-export site.
- Clarifies the zero timestamp test name to describe the actual `updatedAt: 0` input.

## Title precedence

1. `displayName`
2. `subject`
3. explicit `entry.label`
4. `firstUserMessage` (truncated)
5. auto-populated `entry.origin?.label`
6. `sessionId` prefix fallback

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: explicit session labels should survive heartbeat-style transcript summaries, while auto-populated origin labels should not override a real first user message.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on this branch after rebasing onto current `main`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --eval 'import { deriveSessionTitle } from "./src/gateway/session-title.ts";
const cases = {
  explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), label: "My App - Dashboard" }, "heartbeat"),
  transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, "Need help with deployment"),
  originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abc123", updatedAt: Date.now(), origin: { label: "Discord: Engineering" } }, null),
  zeroUpdatedAtFallback: deriveSessionTitle({ sessionId: "abcd1234-5678-90ef-ghij-klmnopqrstuv", updatedAt: 0 }),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(cases, null, 2));'
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "explicitLabelBeatsHeartbeat": "My App - Dashboard",
  "transcriptBeatsAutoOriginLabel": "Need help with deployment",
  "originLabelFallbackWithoutTranscript": "Discord: Engineering",
  "zeroUpdatedAtFallback": "abcd1234"
}
```

Observed result after fix: explicit labels win over heartbeat transcript text; transcript text wins over auto-populated origin labels; origin labels still act as fallback when there is no transcript; zero `updatedAt` keeps the session-id-only fallback.

What was not tested: full gateway UI rendering was not manually opened; this proof covers the extracted title derivation behavior directly.

## Validation

- `pnpm test src/gateway/session-title.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

target.title: fix(gateway): exit 0 on EADDRINUSE when healthy OpenClaw gateway is already running
target.body:
## Problem
When `openclaw gateway run` hits EADDRINUSE, it exits with code 1. Under systemd/launchd with `Restart=on-failure`, this creates a tight crash-loop.

## Root Cause
`GatewayLockError` on port conflict always exited non-zero, even when an already-healthy OpenClaw gateway was listening on that port.

## Fix
On EADDRINUSE, probe the gateway using the correct bind/TLS-aware URL with resolved auth credentials. Exit 0 only when a healthy OpenClaw instance is confirmed. Exit 1 for foreign processes or failed probes.

Probe handles all auth modes:
- **shared-secret** (token/password): forwarded directly
- **trusted-proxy**: synthetic proxy headers derived from config instead of shared-secret credentials
- **TLS with self-signed cert**: fingerprint loaded from `loadGatewayTlsRuntime` and pinned for the probe

Also fixes a pre-existing type error in `src/cli/update-cli.test.ts`.

Supersedes #29499 — thanks @BlueBirdBack for identifying the issue.

---

## AI-assisted

- [x] Built with Claude (Kees/OpenClaw) + Codex
- [x] Fully tested: VM gates (pnpm build + check + vitest gateway-cli + probe)
- [x] Author understands what the code does
- [x] Codex review findings addressed and resolved

target.title: feat(control-ui): add cron calendar timeline view
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a 24h timeline strip to the Cron Jobs page in the Control UI, providing an at-a-glance view of today's scheduled tasks.

Closes #40644

## Screenshots

### Dark Theme — Full View
![Full page dark](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dark-full.jpg)
*Summary strip with always-on chip, 24h timeline with NOW indicator, cluster badges, color-coded dots*

### Cluster Hover — Overlapping Jobs
![Cluster hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/cluster-hover.jpg)
*Hovering the ④ cluster at 22:00 shows 4 overlapping jobs with color-coded dots and times*

### Single Dot Hover — Job Info Popup
![Dot hover](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/dot-hover.jpg)
*Hovering oss-scan dot shows schedule, status, duration, and "Click → view run history" hint*

### Light Theme
![Light theme](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xvchris/cron-calendar-screenshots/main/light-theme.jpg)
*Full light theme support via existing CSS variables*

## Features

### Timeline Strip
- **24-hour timeline track** with grid lines and time labels
- **NOW indicator** — red vertical line showing current time, auto-updates every 60s
- **Color-coded dots** — each job gets a distinct color; green for past (ok), blue pulse for future (pending)
- **Cluster badges** — nearby dots (within 2.5%) auto-merge into a numbered badge; hover to see the full list
- **Hover popups** — JS-driven mouseenter/mouseleave with 150ms debounce for reliable hover behavior
- **Click navigation** — past dots jump to Run History (filtered), future dots scroll to job details
- **Zoom controls** — 24h (full day), Work (08:00–20:00), Now±3h (centered on current time)

### Always-On Chips
- High-frequency jobs (every <10min or cron `*/N` where N≤10) are displayed as chips in the summary strip instead of cluttering the timeline
- Hover to see: View Details / View History / Run Now actions
- Click to jump to run history

### Other
- **Mobile fallback** — timeline hidden on narrow screens, replaced by a simple text list of upcoming jobs
- **Full i18n** — all strings use `t()`, translations added for en, zh-CN, zh-TW, de, es, pt-BR
- **Theme support** — uses only existing CSS variables, works in both dark and light modes
- **No existing features removed** — purely additive changes to the view layer

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `ui/src/ui/helpers/cron-timeline.ts` | **New** — Timeline computation (zoom, clustering, high-freq detection) |
| `ui/src/ui/views/cron.ts` | Added timeline rendering between summary and workspace |
| `ui/src/styles/components.css` | Added timeline CSS (523 lines) |
| `ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts` | Added `cron.timeline.*` keys to all 6 locales |

## Testing

- Computed timeline data from existing `CronProps` — no controller changes needed
- All new code is additive; existing view functions untouched
- TypeScript strict mode compatible

---

*AI Disclosure: This PR was authored with AI assistance (Claude). All code was reviewed and tested by a human.*

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: feat(workspace): add tiered bootstrap loading with configurable bootstrapTier
target.body:
Closes #22438

## Motivation

Bootstrap files consume LLM tokens on every session. For users with large workspaces, loading all files into every session — including sub-agents and cron jobs — wastes context window budget on files the agent never references. This PR introduces tiered loading so users can control how much context each session type receives, **reducing unnecessary token usage** while preserving persona consistency where needed.

## What

Introduces a three-tier bootstrap file loading system (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) with a new `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` configuration option. Both the `minimal` and `standard` tiers exclude hook-loaded extras (e.g. `packages/*/AGENTS.md` from the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook); `full` keeps everything.

## Why

The current binary split (main=all, subagent/cron=minimal) doesn't support use cases where subagents need persona context or where main sessions should be lightweight. This adds a clean, backward-compatible middle ground that lets users optimize their token budget per session type.

## How

- **`resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — resolves the effective tier from session type + optional config override.
- **`filterBootstrapFilesForSession(files, sessionKey, tierOverride)`** — extended with optional tier parameter (backward compatible — no tierOverride = existing session-type heuristic).
- **Hook-source guard** — both `minimal` and `standard` filters check `file.source !== "hook"` before allowlist matching, so a hook-loaded extra whose basename happens to be in the allowlist cannot slip in. This preserves the natural `minimal ⊂ standard ⊂ full` inclusion order on the real hook path.
- **Config** — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` in the zod schema, TypeScript types, labels, and help text.

The `source: "root" | "hook"` provenance field on `WorkspaceBootstrapFile` is the discriminator: root-level bootstrap files keep flowing through `minimal` and `standard`; per-package extras registered via the `bootstrap-extra-files` hook are only reachable through `full`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: tiered bootstrap loading — `agents.defaults.bootstrapTier` (`minimal` | `standard` | `full`) controls which workspace files are injected into each session's context window. Closes #22438. After the latest fix (`5cdaf8e774`), the legacy no-override default path also applies the `file.source !== "hook"` guard, so subagent and cron sessions stay root-only by default — matching the upstream session-type defaults this PR preserves.

Real environment tested: macOS 26.2 on Apple Silicon, Node 24.13.0, pnpm 11.2.2, against the fork branch tip at commit `5cdaf8e774`. A reproduction harness at the repo root (`repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`, not committed) imports the real `src/agents/workspace.ts` from this branch and invokes `resolveBootstrapTier` and `filterBootstrapFilesForSession` against an explicit `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` containing both root-loaded files and hook-loaded extras whose basenames collide with the root allowlist.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. `git checkout 5cdaf8e774` (this PR's head)
2. `pnpm install --prefer-offline --frozen-lockfile`
3. Author the reproduction harness at the repo root, importing `./src/agents/workspace.js` and calling the exported functions directly with a `WorkspaceBootstrapFile[]` shaped like a real workspace (root files + two hook-sourced `packages/*/AGENTS.md` entries).
4. `node ./node_modules/.bin/tsx repro-tiered-bootstrap.mts`

Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture, run live against commit `5cdaf8e774`:

```text

===== resolveBootstrapTier(sessionKey, tierOverride) =====
  (no sessionKey)  default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  main session      default=standard   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  subagent session  default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full
  cron session      default=minimal   override=minimal→minimal   override=standard→standard   override=full→full

===== filterBootstrapFilesForSession — DEFAULTS (no tierOverride) =====
(no sessionKey), no override  →  10 file(s):
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/AGENTS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/TOOLS.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/SOUL.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/IDENTITY.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/USER.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/HEARTBEAT.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/BOOTSTRAP.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/MEMORY.md  [root]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/api/AGENTS.md  [hook]
    - /tmp/proof-workspace/packages/web/AGENTS.md  [hook]
```
